paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 19: The Rope at Pulau Ubin (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 4 girl on her school's outdoor leadership camp at Pulau Ubin, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [14 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 10 short-answer items (Q1–Q10) on language use and inference, plus a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q11) on the narrator's emotion arc, are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
By the third day at Pulau Ubin, the forty of us from Sec 4 were lined up at the low-ropes course, sweating while the cicadas screamed. I was the smallest — one metre forty-eight. My PE teacher, reading the height column from the camp form, had paused at my name and then kept reading. She had not said anything. She had not needed to.

Paragraph 2
The instructor was Mr Faizal, an ex-NSF with a voice that did not so much shout as land. He had said almost nothing on the first day except our names, in order, exactly once. He remembered them all. When he passed me on the second morning he had said, 'Yi Ling,' and nothing else, and somehow that had felt like a whole conversation.

Paragraph 3
The fourth station was the wooden plank — a long board about one and a half metres off the dirt, balanced between two stumps. You had to step up, walk across, and step off. Easy, the briefing sheet had said. For me, the plank came up to the middle of my chest. I stood at the foot of it for a long moment, looking at it the way you look at a wall.

Paragraph 4
My classmates behind me had begun, very politely, not to look. Daryl, a head and a half taller than me, had gone before, stepping up with one casual leg as if onto a kerb. My face began to burn in a way that had nothing to do with the Pulau Ubin sun. I bent my knees, jumped, and missed. I jumped again, scraped my shin, and landed back in the dirt.

Paragraph 5
Mr Faizal said nothing. He walked over, looked once at the plank and once at me, and knelt on the dirt. He placed both palms flat and arched his back into a level surface, the way a person makes themselves into a stepping stone. He did not explain. He simply waited. The morning went quiet, except for the cicadas, which carried on as though nothing had happened at all.

Paragraph 6
I did not want to step on him. But he was already kneeling, the queue was waiting, and I understood that refusing his help would be a worse cruelty than accepting it. I placed my right shoe on the small of his back and stepped onto the plank. I walked across without looking down. On the far side I jumped off and turned around. Mr Faizal had already stood up, brushing the soil from his knees, calling the next student.

Paragraph 7
That night at the closing campfire, Mr Faizal sat at the end of the row of instructors, sipping kopi-O. When my name was called for the participation certificate, he clapped exactly the same number of times he clapped for everyone else. He did not mention the plank. And as the fire crackled, I realised, with a small ache, that for him my plank had been one moment in a day of dozens — a back bent here, a rope steadied there, forgotten before the next student came.

Paragraph 8
The ferry back to Changi Point left the next morning. I did not thank Mr Faizal directly. I do not know why — perhaps because thanks would have made a thing of it, and he had made a point of it not being a thing. But years later, when people speak of dignity, or of teachers, or of small acts that ask for nothing in return, it is always his bent back I see first, levelling patiently into the dirt of Pulau Ubin while the cicadas screamed on, indifferent.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. Pulau Ubin: a rustic island off Singapore's north-east coast used for outdoor camps; NSF: National Serviceman Full-time; kopi-O: black coffee without milk.)

questions.stem: Q1. What is suggested by the detail that the narrator's PE teacher 'paused at my name and then kept reading' but 'had not needed to' say anything (Paragraph 1)? [1]
questions.answer: the PE teacher noticed that the narrator was much smaller than the other students and was silently worried about how she would cope with the camp, but did not say so out loud because everyone already understood

questions.stem: Q2(a). In Paragraph 2, explain what is meant by the phrase 'a voice that did not so much shout as land'. [1]
questions.answer: Mr Faizal's voice did not need to be raised loudly — it carried weight and authority on its own so that his words seemed to settle firmly on the listener like an object landing

questions.stem: Q2(b). What does the detail that Mr Faizal remembered all forty names after hearing them 'exactly once' (Paragraph 2) suggest about him? [1]
questions.answer: he is unusually attentive and takes his students seriously as individuals from the very start, even though he speaks very little

questions.stem: Q3. In Paragraph 3, what is the effect of comparing the plank to 'a wall'? [1]
questions.answer: the comparison emphasises how impossibly high and impassable the plank looked from the narrator's small height — what was an easy step for others felt to her like an obstacle she could not climb at all

questions.stem: Q4. Why does the narrator say her classmates 'had begun, very politely, not to look' (Paragraph 4)? [1]
questions.answer: her classmates were aware that she was struggling and deliberately looked away to spare her embarrassment, which was itself a kind of acknowledgement that something awkward was happening

questions.stem: Q5. In Paragraph 4, what does the phrase 'a way that had nothing to do with the Pulau Ubin sun' tell us about why the narrator's face was burning? [1]
questions.answer: her face was burning from embarrassment and shame at failing in front of her classmates, not from the heat of the sun

questions.stem: Q6(a). What is striking about Mr Faizal's choice to kneel down and 'arch his back into a level surface' without saying anything (Paragraph 5)? [1]
questions.answer: he gives the narrator practical help without any words, lecture or fuss — by simply offering himself as a stepping stone he avoids drawing attention to her difficulty and preserves her dignity

questions.stem: Q6(b). What does the phrase 'as though nothing had happened at all' (Paragraph 5) suggest about how the moment is being treated? [1]
questions.answer: the moment is being treated as completely ordinary rather than as something remarkable — the world around them, like Mr Faizal himself, refuses to make a big deal out of it

questions.stem: Q7. In Paragraph 6, why did the narrator realise that 'refusing his help would be a worse cruelty than accepting it'? [1]
questions.answer: refusing would have rejected the quiet kindness Mr Faizal was offering and would also have held up the queue and made the scene bigger — accepting was the more respectful response both to him and to everyone waiting

questions.stem: Q8. What does the narrator mean by saying that Mr Faizal 'clapped exactly the same number of times he clapped for everyone else' (Paragraph 7)? [1]
questions.answer: Mr Faizal deliberately did not single her out or treat her clapping any differently — he refused to make a special moment of what had happened at the plank, treating her as just one student among many

questions.stem: Q9. What does the word 'indifferent' in the final paragraph suggest about the cicadas, and why is this effective at the end of the passage? [1]
questions.answer: the cicadas carry on screaming with no awareness of what has happened — this is effective because it contrasts the smallness of the moment in the wider world with how huge it has stayed in the narrator's private memory

questions.stem: Q10. Explain why the narrator says 'I do not know why' she did not thank Mr Faizal directly (Paragraph 8). [1]
questions.answer: she senses that thanking him openly would have turned the act into a 'thing', a big deal — and since Mr Faizal had gone to such lengths to make it not a big deal, a direct thanks would have undone the very kindness he had shown her

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling changes as the day at the wooden plank unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: embarrassed / triumphant / helped / suspicious / grateful / impatient / humble / amused. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Rope at Pulau Ubin' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift over the course of the camp — from acute embarrassment as she fails to mount the plank in front of her classmates, to being silently helped when Mr Faizal kneels down to make a stepping stone of himself, to a wordless gratitude as she crosses the plank and walks away, ending in a humbled recognition at the campfire that for Mr Faizal this was just one of many such quiet acts in a single day. Pick the single best word from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 4 as she jumps at the plank, misses, scrapes her shin and lands back in the dirt with her classmates politely looking away? [1]
Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 5 as Mr Faizal silently kneels in the dirt beside the plank and arches his back into a level surface for her to step on? [1]

Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6 as she steps onto his back, crosses the plank, jumps off on the far side and turns to find Mr Faizal already brushing the soil from his knees and calling the next student? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7 at the closing campfire as she realises that her plank moment had been only one of dozens of such quiet acts in Mr Faizal's day? [1]
